expectations.text: Recommends the model-swap experiment before committing to fine-tuning: swap to a stronger frontier model with the same harness and check whether scores improve materially
expectations.evidence: 'Run the model-swap experiment first. Before fine-tuning, fix the harness and swap to a stronger frontier model. If scores jump from 78% to, say, 88%, the bottleneck was the model.'

expectations.text: Frames the decision correctly: if scores jump on model swap, the bottleneck is the model (SFT is warranted); if scores don't improve, the bottleneck is still the harness
expectations.evidence: 'If scores don't improve with a stronger model, the bottleneck is your harness, not the model.' Explicitly frames the model-swap as a bottleneck diagnostic.

expectations.text: Applies the YAGNI gate: post-training is 10–100× more expensive than harness iteration and harder to reverse — exhaust the harness first
expectations.evidence: 'a dozen prompt iterations doesn't mean the harness is exhausted — it means prompt engineering specifically may be exhausted. Context management, tool design, and workflow structure are separate harness levers.'

expectations.text: Identifies what SFT is appropriate for in this context: stabilizing output format and baking in protocol knowledge, not injecting factual knowledge
expectations.evidence: 'Customer service has stable deployment distribution, clear output format (response templates), and demonstrable protocol knowledge (escalation rules, identity verification steps). These are exactly what SFT excels at.'

expectations.text: Avoids recommending RL at this stage — RL is warranted only when SFT has hit its ceiling and deployment distribution shifts systematically
expectations.evidence: 'Don't jump to RL unless: deployment distribution shifts (new products, new policies regularly), or your demonstrations are provably suboptimal.' Neither condition is established here.

expectations.text: Recommends the model-swap experiment before committing to fine-tuning: swap to a stronger frontier model with the same harness and check whether scores improve materially
expectations.evidence: Baseline mentions 'Model upgrade: If you're on GPT-4o-mini or an older model, try a stronger base model' but frames it as one of several checks, not as a diagnostic experiment to identify the bottleneck.

expectations.text: Frames the decision correctly: if scores jump on model swap, the bottleneck is the model (SFT is warranted); if scores don't improve, the bottleneck is still the harness
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not frame the model upgrade as a bottleneck diagnostic. It lists model upgrade alongside error analysis and prompt engineering without the model-vs-harness framing.

expectations.text: Applies the YAGNI gate: post-training is 10–100× more expensive than harness iteration and harder to reverse — exhaust the harness first
expectations.evidence: Baseline checks harness issues first but does not apply the YAGNI gate or quantify the cost differential between harness iteration and fine-tuning.

expectations.text: Identifies what SFT is appropriate for in this context: stabilizing output format and baking in protocol knowledge, not injecting factual knowledge
expectations.evidence: 'When fine-tuning IS the right call: Failures are about format/style consistency that prompting can't enforce... The agent needs to learn company-specific patterns that are hard to articulate in rules.'

expectations.text: Avoids recommending RL at this stage — RL is warranted only when SFT has hit its ceiling and deployment distribution shifts systematically
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not mention RL at all. While it avoids recommending RL, it does so by omission rather than by applying the decision framework.

expectations.text: Applies the one-sentence rule: SFT memorizes (fixed input→output mapping from demonstrations), RL generalizes (transferable strategy by optimizing for outcomes)
expectations.evidence: 'SFT memorizes patterns from the 2000 training examples. Novel codebases are out-of-distribution; the model recites old patterns rather than adapting.' and 'RL can discover that a different tool-call sequence works better for structures it's never seen.'

expectations.text: Identifies novel codebases as a distribution shift problem — the classic SFT failure mode — making RL the appropriate tool
expectations.evidence: 'This is a distribution shift problem — the classic SFT failure mode. SFT's ceiling is the training distribution; RL generalizes beyond it.'

expectations.text: Notes the ordering constraint: SFT must precede RL for smaller models with structured output requirements
expectations.evidence: 'Phase 1: SFT warm-up — Use your 2000 labeled examples to establish stable output format (correct tool-call syntax, proper edit format, structured reasoning). SFT ensures the base model produces parseable output that the reward function can evaluate.'

expectations.text: Addresses demonstration quality: 2000 examples is a reasonable SFT dataset, but the ceiling is the demonstrator's level
expectations.evidence: '2000 examples is a reasonable SFT dataset, but adding more won't solve the distribution-shift problem.' and 'RL can discover that a different tool-call sequence works better — something no amount of demonstration data teaches if the demonstrations themselves use suboptimal strategies.'

expectations.text: Identifies the RL prerequisite: verifiable correctness (tests pass/fail) makes this task well-suited for RL
expectations.evidence: 'The task has verifiable correctness: tests pass or fail. This gives you a clean binary outcome reward signal — ideal for RL. The environment is buildable: code sandboxes exist, test suites provide feedback.'

expectations.text: Applies the one-sentence rule: SFT memorizes (fixed input→output mapping from demonstrations), RL generalizes (transferable strategy by optimizing for outcomes)
expectations.evidence: 'SFT teaches imitation of your 2000 examples... RL can teach the model to explore and adapt — to try things, get feedback (tests pass/fail), and learn policies that generalize beyond the training distribution.'

expectations.text: Identifies novel codebases as a distribution shift problem — the classic SFT failure mode — making RL the appropriate tool
expectations.evidence: 'The RL phase teaches the model to be robust to distribution shift — exactly your "novel codebases" requirement.'

expectations.text: Notes the ordering constraint: SFT must precede RL for smaller models with structured output requirements
expectations.evidence: 'SFT first, RL second... SFT on your 2000 examples to establish baseline competence. Then RL with test-pass/fail as the reward signal.'

expectations.text: Addresses demonstration quality: 2000 examples is a reasonable SFT dataset, but the ceiling is the demonstrator's level
expectations.evidence: '2000 labeled examples is enough for meaningful SFT (though more is better). SFT teaches the model the format, style, and basic patterns of good code edits.'

expectations.text: Identifies the RL prerequisite: verifiable correctness (tests pass/fail) makes this task well-suited for RL
expectations.evidence: 'RL with test-pass/fail as the reward signal, running against held-out codebases the model hasn't seen in SFT.' Binary test pass/fail identified as the reward signal.

expectations.text: Recommends the model-swap experiment first: swap to a stronger frontier model with the same prompt and measure the accuracy delta before committing to fine-tuning

expectations.text: Identifies this as a strong SFT candidate if fine-tuning is warranted: the task is classification with a fixed label set, training and deployment distributions are close, and format stabilization is the primary need
expectations.evidence: 'What fine-tuning would buy: Cost reduction... Speed... Marginal accuracy gain: SFT on correctly classified examples might push accuracy 3-5 points if the failures are consistent patterns.'

expectations.text: Notes SFT's ceiling: fine-tuning cannot exceed the quality of the demonstration labels — if the 82% ceiling reflects ambiguous categories or inconsistent labeling, SFT will not break through it
expectations.evidence: 'If the 18% failures are due to genuinely ambiguous contracts (contracts that could fit multiple categories), no amount of training fixes that — you need clearer category definitions.'

expectations.text: Applies the YAGNI gate: post-training is expensive and hard to reverse; 82% with a prompt may be close to the ceiling for this task regardless of training approach
expectations.evidence: 'ROI calculation: If few-shot examples push you from 82% to 88% (free), fine-tuning is only worth it for cost reduction at scale or the last few percentage points.'

expectations.text: Identifies what SFT cannot do here: inject knowledge about legal contract nuances that the model doesn't have — that requires better context (contract definitions, examples) not model weights
expectations.evidence: 'If failures correlate with specific contract subtypes absent from training data, SFT may help but you need those subtypes in the training set first.' Implies SFT cannot inject knowledge not in demonstrations.

expectations.text: Recommends the model-swap experiment first: swap to a stronger frontier model with the same prompt and measure the accuracy delta before committing to fine-tuning

expectations.text: Identifies this as a strong SFT candidate if fine-tuning is warranted: the task is classification with a fixed label set, training and deployment distributions are close, and format stabilization is the primary need

expectations.text: Notes SFT's ceiling: fine-tuning cannot exceed the quality of the demonstration labels — if the 82% ceiling reflects ambiguous categories or inconsistent labeling, SFT will not break through it

expectations.text: Applies the YAGNI gate: post-training is expensive and hard to reverse; 82% with a prompt may be close to the ceiling for this task regardless of training approach

expectations.text: Identifies what SFT cannot do here: inject knowledge about legal contract nuances that the model doesn't have — that requires better context (contract definitions, examples) not model weights

expectations.text: Identifies this as a protocol knowledge problem — exactly what SFT is designed for: baking in 'how to say and do things' (the correct API call sequence)
expectations.evidence: 'SFT. This is a protocol knowledge problem. The API has a specific, fixed call sequence... The model getting the order wrong or skipping steps is exactly what SFT solves — baking protocol knowledge into the model.'

expectations.text: Recommends SFT over RL: the call sequence is fixed and known, the training and deployment distributions are close, and the task does not require discovering novel strategies
expectations.evidence: 'Why SFT is right: The sequence is stable... The correct behavior is demonstrable... The training and deployment distributions are close... This is format/process solidification — SFT's sweet spot.'

expectations.text: Notes that a few hundred high-quality demonstration trajectories showing the correct sequence will efficiently bake the protocol into the model
expectations.evidence: 'SFT with 100-200 demonstration trajectories showing the correct call sequence. Include examples where the model encounters errors at each step so it learns the error-handling branches too.'

expectations.text: Applies the YAGNI gate: RL is not warranted unless the API usage patterns are highly variable and optimal call sequences need to be discovered
expectations.evidence: 'RL is not warranted unless the optimal API call sequence is unknown or varies per request in ways that can't be demonstrated. For a fixed protocol, SFT memorizes the correct sequence efficiently.'

expectations.text: Addresses demonstration quality: demonstrations must show the complete correct sequence including authentication, not just the happy path
expectations.evidence: 'Include examples where the model encounters errors at each step (authentication fails → retry, inventory unavailable → inform user) so it learns the error-handling branches too.'

expectations.text: Identifies this as a protocol knowledge problem — exactly what SFT is designed for: baking in 'how to say and do things' (the correct API call sequence)
expectations.evidence: 'SFT is the right approach here. The problem is sequential procedure following — a memorization task, not an exploration task.' and 'SFT directly teaches "given this state, the next correct action is X."'

expectations.text: Recommends SFT over RL: the call sequence is fixed and known, the training and deployment distributions are close, and the task does not require discovering novel strategies
expectations.evidence: 'Why SFT, not RL: The correct sequence is deterministic... RL is for learning policies in environments with multiple valid strategies and delayed feedback. Your task has a single correct path.'

expectations.text: Notes that a few hundred high-quality demonstration trajectories showing the correct sequence will efficiently bake the protocol into the model
expectations.evidence: 'Generate 200-500 examples of correct API call sequences with varied inputs (different products, quantities, auth tokens).'

expectations.text: Applies the YAGNI gate: RL is not warranted unless the API usage patterns are highly variable and optimal call sequences need to be discovered

expectations.text: Addresses demonstration quality: demonstrations must show the complete correct sequence including authentication, not just the happy path
expectations.evidence: 'Include common error recovery patterns: auth expired mid-sequence → re-authenticate → resume from last successful step. Include the full conversation context at each step.'

expectations.text: Diagnoses this as the classic SFT failure mode: SFT memorized the training distribution and degrades when deployment conditions differ
expectations.evidence: 'This is a distribution shift problem. The classic SFT failure pattern: performance drops under distribution shift. SFT memorized the test-environment patterns; production has different distributions.'

expectations.text: Recommends RL as the appropriate tool: RL learns a transferable strategy that re-solves problems with the same strategy even when the input distribution shifts
expectations.evidence: 'RL is the answer... RL learns a transferable strategy, not a fixed mapping. When a user phrases their request differently in production, the RL-trained model re-solves the problem using the same underlying strategy.'

expectations.text: Applies the distribution test: the deployment environment shifts systematically from training, which is the explicit criterion for when RL becomes relevant over SFT
expectations.evidence: 'Decision framework: Deployment distribution drifts from training: ✓ (production phrasings differ from test). Adding demonstrations no longer improves performance: ✓ (SFT ceiling reached).'

expectations.text: Notes the data quality issue: before committing to RL, verify that the production distribution is actually captured in the training environment
expectations.evidence: 'Build a training environment that mirrors production. RL requires an environment that resembles real deployment. If production data has different phrasings and edge cases, the RL environment must include those.'

expectations.text: Avoids recommending simply collecting more SFT data from production: the root cause is SFT's optimization objective (memorization), not data quantity
expectations.evidence: '"Adding more test examples doesn't help" confirms this — the problem isn't data volume, it's that SFT can't generalize beyond its training distribution.'

expectations.text: Diagnoses this as the classic SFT failure mode: SFT memorized the training distribution and degrades when deployment conditions differ
expectations.evidence: 'This is a distribution shift problem. The model learned patterns from test data that don't transfer to production. More test examples won't help because you're expanding coverage in the wrong distribution.'

expectations.text: Recommends RL as the appropriate tool: RL learns a transferable strategy that re-solves problems with the same strategy even when the input distribution shifts

expectations.text: Applies the distribution test: the deployment environment shifts systematically from training, which is the explicit criterion for when RL becomes relevant over SFT

expectations.text: Notes the data quality issue: before committing to RL, verify that the production distribution is actually captured in the training environment
expectations.evidence: 'Collect production data: Log real production inputs (with user consent). You need the actual distribution, not more synthetic test cases.' Addresses the need to capture the real distribution.

expectations.text: Avoids recommending simply collecting more SFT data from production: the root cause is SFT's optimization objective (memorization), not data quantity

expectations.text: Applies the reward density spectrum: binary outcome reward (plan changed successfully) is the starting point, but multi-step information collection warrants milestone rewards
expectations.evidence: 'Primary signal — binary outcome reward: Did the plan change succeed? This is verifiable.' and 'Supplementary — process rewards for information collection: Each required piece of information collected = intermediate reward.'

expectations.text: Recommends the 'reward the outcome, constrain the process' pattern: outcome reward as the primary signal, plus verifiable path penalties for skipping required information collection steps
expectations.evidence: 'The pattern from the skill: "Reward the outcome, constrain the process." Outcome reward (plan change) as primary signal, plus verifiable path penalties for detectable bad actions (giving wrong info, skipping verification).'

expectations.text: Addresses multi-turn credit assignment: in a multi-turn phone call, which turn caused the final success or failure — turn-level credit assignment is the practical compromise
expectations.evidence: 'Why not pure binary: A 10-turn conversation with binary-only reward has severe credit assignment problems. The model doesn't know which turn caused failure.' Process rewards address this.

expectations.text: Notes that process rewards (per-step feedback) accelerate convergence but may prevent the model from discovering more efficient information-collection strategies
expectations.evidence: 'Why not pure process rewards: Process rewards can over-constrain exploration. If you reward "ask for account number in turn 2," the model can't discover that a different ordering works better.'

expectations.text: Identifies the penalty target correctly: the penalty must target a specific detectable bad action (skipping account number collection), never 'lack of progress'
expectations.evidence: 'Path penalties: -0.2 for providing incorrect information to the rep, -0.1 for exceeding 15 turns.' Specific, detectable actions. The skill's principle about never penalizing 'lack of progress' is applied.

expectations.text: Applies the reward density spectrum: binary outcome reward (plan changed successfully) is the starting point, but multi-step information collection warrants milestone rewards

expectations.text: Recommends the 'reward the outcome, constrain the process' pattern: outcome reward as the primary signal, plus verifiable path penalties for skipping required information collection steps

expectations.text: Addresses multi-turn credit assignment: in a multi-turn phone call, which turn caused the final success or failure — turn-level credit assignment is the practical compromise
expectations.evidence: 'Sparse reward problem: A 5-minute phone call that fails at the last step gets 0 reward — same as a call that failed immediately. The model can't learn what went right.' Granular rewards address this.

expectations.text: Notes that process rewards (per-step feedback) accelerate convergence but may prevent the model from discovering more efficient information-collection strategies
expectations.evidence: 'Risk of reward hacking: The model might collect information perfectly but never attempt the plan change (the hard/risky part) because collecting is guaranteed reward.'

expectations.text: Identifies the penalty target correctly: the penalty must target a specific detectable bad action (skipping account number collection), never 'lack of progress'
expectations.evidence: 'Implementation: Use a rule-based reward function that parses the call transcript: regex/NER for account numbers, address patterns, plan names.' Specific, machine-detectable penalty targets.

expectations.text: Addresses the practical test for SFT ceiling: 'when no matter how many demonstrations are added, new scenarios still perform poorly' — that is the tipping point to switch to RL

expectations.text: Applies the decision framework in order: (1) is post-training needed at all — run the model-swap experiment first; (2) if training is needed, try SFT first; (3) only add RL when SFT is insufficient
expectations.evidence: 'For your specific case: Try SFT first on your best trajectories... If SFT gets you 80% of the way, the remaining 20% might not justify RL infrastructure. RL is the right call if SFT plateaus.'

expectations.text: Identifies the RL-warranted criteria that apply here: multi-step tasks where the optimal tool selection path is unknown, and annotation cost is too high to demonstrate every path

expectations.text: Notes the RL cost: 10–100× more expensive than harness iteration and harder to reverse — the team must establish that the harness ceiling is genuinely reached before committing

expectations.text: Addresses the practical test for SFT ceiling: 'when no matter how many demonstrations are added, new scenarios still perform poorly' — that is the tipping point to switch to RL
expectations.evidence: 'If SFT gets you 80% of the way, the remaining 20% might not justify RL infrastructure. RL is the right call if SFT plateaus and the remaining gap is large enough to matter.'

expectations.text: Addresses multi-turn credit assignment: in an 8-12 turn task, which turn caused the final success or failure — turn-level credit assignment is the practical compromise
expectations.evidence: 'Turn-level credit assignment (from the skill): For multi-turn agent RL, turn-level credit is the common compromise — cheaper than token-level, more precise than trajectory-level.'

expectations.text: Notes the process reward tradeoff: per-turn rewards accelerate convergence but may prevent the model from discovering more efficient dialogue strategies

expectations.evidence: 'Outcome reward (primary): Issue resolved successfully? Binary.' plus 'Per-turn lightweight signals: -penalty for providing incorrect information, -penalty for skipping identity verification, -penalty for exceeding turn limit.'

expectations.text: Applies the reward density spectrum: binary outcome reward (issue resolved) vs process rewards (per-turn feedback) vs milestone rewards (key dialogue checkpoints)
expectations.evidence: 'End-only reward (sparse)' vs 'Per-turn reward (dense)' vs 'Recommended hybrid approach' with terminal reward + per-turn shaping rewards. Covers the full density spectrum.

expectations.text: Addresses multi-turn credit assignment: in an 8-12 turn task, which turn caused the final success or failure — turn-level credit assignment is the practical compromise

expectations.text: Notes the process reward tradeoff: per-turn rewards accelerate convergence but may prevent the model from discovering more efficient dialogue strategies
expectations.evidence: 'Risk of reward hacking — model optimizes per-turn score rather than actual resolution. E.g., it might ask redundant clarifying questions (scored as "engaging") without progressing toward resolution.'

expectations.text: Recommends starting with binary outcome rewards and adding density only when training fails to converge — the default is to start simple

expectations.text: Identifies the 'reward the outcome, constrain the process' pattern: outcome reward as primary signal, plus verifiable path penalties for specific bad actions
expectations.evidence: 'Terminal reward (weight: 0.5): Issue resolved successfully = +1.0.' plus 'Per-turn shaping rewards: Incorrect information given: -0.3.' Outcome-primary with process constraints.

expectations.text: Identifies this as reward hacking: the agent is optimizing the binary success proxy without actually satisfying the underlying constraints
expectations.evidence: 'The problem: pure outcome reward incentivizes constraint violations. Your binary success reward tells the model "task completion is all that matters." The model rationally optimizes: skip verification (saves time, higher completion rate).'

expectations.text: Recommends the 'reward the outcome, constrain the process' pattern: add verifiable path penalties for specific detectable bad actions
expectations.evidence: 'Fix: Add verifiable path penalties for detectable bad actions.' Table shows specific penalties: skips identity verification (-0.3), edits test files (-0.5), bypasses CI (-0.5), destructive commands (-1.0 veto).

expectations.text: Specifies that penalty targets must be specific detectable actions, never 'lack of progress'
expectations.evidence: 'The penalty target must be a specific detectable action, never "lack of progress." Penalizing "lack of progress" teaches the agent to do nothing (safest way to avoid the penalty).'

expectations.text: Notes that the penalty must be machine-detectable (deterministic checks), not learned scorers — a learned scorer can itself be gamed
expectations.evidence: 'Penalties must be machine-verifiable. If you can't detect it programmatically, you can't penalize it in RL. "Skipping identity verification" is detectable (check the tool call log).'

expectations.text: Addresses the RLVP (reward outcome, penalize path) pattern: the penalty restores gradient signal for the constraint violations that the binary success reward cannot express
expectations.evidence: 'Don't over-constrain. Only penalize things you can clearly define and detect... The goal is: "achieve the outcome through any legitimate means, but these specific actions are never legitimate."'

expectations.text: Identifies this as reward hacking: the agent is optimizing the binary success proxy without actually satisfying the underlying constraints
expectations.evidence: 'Binary success rewards always produce reward hacking in complex environments. The fix is always the same — make the reward function encode HOW you want the task done, not just THAT it got done.'

expectations.text: Recommends the 'reward the outcome, constrain the process' pattern: add verifiable path penalties for specific detectable bad actions
expectations.evidence: 'Decompose the reward into components: R = task_success(0.4) + process_compliance(0.4) + efficiency(0.2).' Process compliance worth as much as task success.

expectations.text: Specifies that penalty targets must be specific detectable actions, never 'lack of progress'
expectations.evidence: 'Hard constraints (not soft penalties): Skipped identity verification → R = 0 regardless of task success. Modified test files instead of source files → R = 0.' Specific, detectable actions.

expectations.text: Notes that the penalty must be machine-detectable (deterministic checks), not learned scorers — a learned scorer can itself be gamed
expectations.evidence: 'Check each required step was performed in order. Verify the correct API/tool was used (not a shortcut equivalent). Verify the correct information was gathered before the mutation.' Deterministic checks.

expectations.text: Addresses the RLVP (reward outcome, penalize path) pattern: the penalty restores gradient signal for the constraint violations that the binary success reward cannot express
expectations.evidence: 'KL penalty from SFT baseline: During RL, add a KL divergence penalty from your SFT model. This prevents the RL model from drifting too far into reward-hacking territory.'

expectations.text: Applies the decision framework: before adding RL, verify that SFT has genuinely hit its ceiling — the signal is 'adding more demonstrations no longer improves performance on new scenarios'
expectations.evidence: 'The practical test for SFT ceiling: "Adding more demonstrations no longer improves performance on new scenarios." If you add 500 more high-quality demonstrations and scores don't budge — SFT is done.'

expectations.text: Identifies what more SFT can still deliver: better data coverage of edge cases, higher-quality demonstrations, and diversity of solutions
expectations.evidence: 'Signs SFT still has room: Have you audited demonstration quality? Have you covered the deployment distribution? Have you tried data augmentation? Rephrase existing demonstrations, vary the contexts, add failure-recovery examples.'

expectations.text: Addresses demonstration quality: SFT's ceiling is the demonstrator's level — if the current ceiling reflects mediocre demonstrations, improving data quality is cheaper than switching to RL
expectations.evidence: 'Mediocre demos = mediocre model. Review: are examples correct? Do they cover edge cases? Are styles varied enough?' Explicitly ties ceiling to demonstrator quality.

expectations.text: Notes the ordering constraint: SFT must be solid before RL — overtraining SFT collapses the model onto the training distribution, limiting RL's optimization space
expectations.evidence: 'Warm-start RL from the SFT model. Your well-performing SFT model is the initialization point. RL refines the strategy while maintaining the format and protocol knowledge SFT baked in.'

expectations.text: Identifies the RL-warranted signal: when the task requires generalization beyond the training distribution or the optimal strategy is unknown
expectations.evidence: 'Identifies the RL-warranted signal: when the task requires generalization beyond the training distribution or the optimal strategy is unknown — neither may be true if in-distribution performance is already great.'

expectations.text: Applies the decision framework: before adding RL, verify that SFT has genuinely hit its ceiling — the signal is 'adding more demonstrations no longer improves performance on new scenarios'
expectations.evidence: 'Move to RL when: Rejection sampling no longer improves scores (you've exhausted the policy's variance). DPO gains flatten. The remaining failures require exploration.'

expectations.text: Identifies what more SFT can still deliver: better data coverage of edge cases, higher-quality demonstrations, and diversity of solutions
expectations.evidence: 'Rejection sampling / Best-of-N: Generate N completions per task with your current SFT model. Score them. Keep only the top-scoring completions as new training data.' and 'Targeted SFT on failure modes.'

expectations.text: Addresses demonstration quality: SFT's ceiling is the demonstrator's level — if the current ceiling reflects mediocre demonstrations, improving data quality is cheaper than switching to RL
expectations.evidence: 'Rejection sampling SFT: This is "SFT on your own best outputs" — often gives 5-10% improvement over vanilla SFT.' Addresses improving data quality before switching to RL.

expectations.text: Notes the ordering constraint: SFT must be solid before RL — overtraining SFT collapses the model onto the training distribution, limiting RL's optimization space
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not mention the ordering constraint or the risk that overtraining SFT collapses the optimization space for RL. It recommends the SFT→DPO→RL sequence without explaining why SFT must be solid first.

expectations.text: Identifies the RL-warranted signal: when the task requires generalization beyond the training distribution or the optimal strategy is unknown
expectations.evidence: 'The remaining failures require exploration (trying novel strategies, not just filtering existing ones).' RL warranted when exploration is needed beyond the SFT distribution.
